column1: Penelope Umbrico’s project Solar Eclipses is made up of a collection of collages created using images of solar eclipses found in the New York Public Library Picture Collection. She creates these collages by placing the images on a scanner and arranging them to get as many as possible into each copy. This book is a collection of photocopies of those original images created on the library scanner. Umbrico’s approach to the project mediates on the duality between physical experience and the digitising of images printed on paper. The process of searching through the library’s collection today seems absurd in light of the facility and accessibility brought by the digital internet age
column2: thus as the collection is gradually digitised
column3: the material form of paper - a reflective medium requiring an external light source to be seen
column4: and that often represents light in the image it supports - is now seen depicted online via the medium of the screen. Reflective paper becomes projective light. Umbrico chooses to focus on the solar eclipse at the library as the epitome of this relationship between the reflective and projective. The dynamic of the eclipse is the negation of natural light with that whose visibility is dependent on it. Thus her process reflects the same fascination with the photocopier itself as an “eclipser”: its light escapes around the paper it scans
column5: creating the densest black – an inverted eclipse. White becomes light

column2: produced mostly in the 1970s and 1980s

column4: Development and Engineering Center near Boston
column5: Massachusetts. The center is still operational today
column6: and employs both military personnel and civilian contractors in its mission to improve the daily lives—and diets—of American soldiers.

column1: For the world’s most formidable army
column2: feeding the troops is fraught with logistical
column3: psychological and food safety challenges. Bacteria is an enemy; supply chains are vital
column4: intricate delivery systems. The goal is to provide sustenance and boost morale across the full range of terrain and troop configurations
column5: from mess halls for the officers and selfservice buffets for the rank-and-file to battlefield canteens and survival rations for commandos behind enemy lines.

column1: Solving this logistical puzzle is like trying to stuff a square peg into a round hole: once you have devised ways to optimize the daily nutritional requirements of the bodies in question
column2: you need to work out the best solutions for preserving and transporting the food
column3: and still guarantee a minimum level of flavor to keep the soldiers happy.

column1: Dominique is the most popular gender-neutral first name in France. It is also the twenty-seventh most common first name
column2: just as Lambert is the twenty-seventh most common last name. From these names Stephanie Solinas establishes her study population of the one hundred and ninety-one Dominique Lamberts listed in the telephone directory (the French white pages). Implementing an array of techniques used by public official services to represent a person’s identity
column3: involving experts
column4: and advancing her investigation over multiple successive stages
column5: Stéphanie Solinas explores and exhausts the processes and tools of representation itself
column6: uncovering of the worlds of each Dominique Lambert. This book displays for the first time a collection of faces within a sample population through which both clichés and singularities are questioned to make up the full portrait of how indeterminate and complex identity appears.";https://rvb-books.com/cdn/shop/products/packshot_a4a73828-daa5-486a-add8-f1d202d24ccc_{width}x.jpg?v=1605615288;https://rvb-books.com/cdn/shop/products/couverture1copie_{width}x.jpg?v=1605615289|https://rvb-books.com/cdn/shop/products/interieur1copie_dbb9e42a-c0a5-489d-9c39-e1021b14a6a5_{width}x.jpg?v=1605615289|https://rvb-books.com/cdn/shop/products/interieur2copie_63e126bf-1a5e-4067-9d86-76bb3d6c59d6_{width}x.jpg?v=1605615289|https://rvb-books.com/cdn/shop/products/interieur3copie_81e61e73-318e-4bc4-8b8b-df41202b3139_{width}x.jpg?v=1605615289|https://rvb-books.com/cdn/shop/products/interieur4copie_2c3328ea-79b6-4e15-9cdd-3cf7d5f0e225_{width}x.jpg?v=1605615289;sold-out;https://rvb-books.com/products/stephanie-solinas-dominique-lambert

column2: Anouk Kruithof and two assistants went to Wall Street in New York City and built a temporary installation of 14 framed prints of different sizes on the edge of the city’s pavement. The prints looked like pixelated monochromes
column3: but were in fact illustrations blown up to a maximum size (3200% in Photoshop) of images found by using Google
column4: searching the word “stress”. Anouk Kruithof asked pedestrians to look at the installation and then had conversations about the pixelated monochromes
column5: the meaning of this project and the potential interpretations of the work. Kruithof asked the people involved if they would like to buy a print
column6: both engaging a commercial gesture and condemning the scarcity of the city dwellers encounterings. She sold 8 of the 14 prints bought by 7 participants when the day’s rain warded off further efforts. Kruithof is not allowed to conduct monetary transactions
column7: so that once a participant told her a price for the print
column8: she actually gave it away for free

column2: Penelope Umberico creates a unique reflection on the 2008 financial crisis in artists book form. column1: In geology an erratic is a rock that differs in type from those around it
column2: having been carried over large distances by long-vanished glaciers. Harvey-Regan’s eponymous series questions the medium of photography and its ability to extract from contexts and alter perception. Originating in the artist’s desire to liberate himself from the weight of representational imagery - a yearning for abstraction - alongside a converse desire to engage with appearances as only photography can
column3: The Erratics began as a series of large format photographs in the Egyptian desert
column4: capturing chalk monoliths in the most objective manner possible. These images were followed by months of meticulously carving and photographing chalk rocks in the studio
column5: forcing a geometry and line to shape the objects towards the photographic surfaces they eventually become. column1: TWO DONKEYS IN A WAR ZONE;Clément Lambelet;FALSE;"24 x 34 cm
The work Two Donkeys in a War Zone finds its source in a video of the U.S. Army available on Youtube. A drone follows an attack against an Isis camp. Between two explosions
column2: the infrared camera briefly highlights two donkeys. This intrusion of two animals unintentionally witnessing human violence had me look for drone strike videos produced by the U.S.
column3: Afghan or British army with moments or details that do not belong to the combat but are instead a part of ""normal life""
column4: the off- camera’s of an asymmetrical war. These photographs of operative videos
column5: supporting military propaganda
column6: show the conflict through the drone’s eye. The operator is only looking for his target
column7: but life carries on next to the explosion. These movements
column8: instants of existence
column9: signify humanity. Two donkeys in a war zone is a search
column10: by cropping and subverting operative images
column11: for traces of life inside pictures of death.";https://rvb-books.com/cdn/shop/products/packshot_3306f49b-f394-4d34-92d2-816454d2b7de_{width}x.jpg?v=1606130329;https://rvb-books.com/cdn/shop/products/interieur10copie_c5c5bc52-36f5-4caa-80ac-1791292a1ee6_{width}x.jpg?v=1606130329|https://rvb-books.com/cdn/shop/products/interieur9copie_46578db9-8a26-4739-93e7-a00f03bb55e6_{width}x.jpg?v=1606130329|https://rvb-books.com/cdn/shop/products/interieur8copie_1473abc1-953f-4109-8028-509f7a471657_{width}x.jpg?v=1606130329|https://rvb-books.com/cdn/shop/products/interieur7copie_9907029b-5313-4439-a5f6-72079a1b6a63_{width}x.jpg?v=1606130329|https://rvb-books.com/cdn/shop/products/interieur6copie_41a9236a-47aa-4d36-b639-a062f56b00ef_{width}x.jpg?v=1606130329;sold-out;https://rvb-books.com/products/quentin-lacombe-two-donkeys-in-a-war-zone

column2: Erik Kessels' father suffered a stroke and has since been barely able to speak or move. Prior to this
column3: he was extremely active. His projects included restoring examples of the iconic Fiat 500 or “Topolino”. Before his stroke
column4: he had completed four such restorations and was working on a fifth
column5: the half-finished carapace of which remains at his home. With the Unfinished Father project Erik Kessels plans to transport this last Topolino to Reggio Emilia and show it alongside the images his father made to document his restoration. For the artist
column6: this work is about a man who
column7: like his vehicle will never be complete destined to forever remain unfinished. Simultaneously
column8: Topolino is the reverse of his father's philosophy in life: he was a man who didn't like to leave things unfinished
column9: who saw them to their end. While films and stories teach us that endings are neat (and often happy)
column10: the truth is that everything ends abruptly
column11: more or less unfinished. No matter how carefully we plan and how precisely we those plans are executed
column12: there is never a guarantee of reaching the desired conclusion. We can thus attempt to control our circumstances
column13: but in the end they control us.";https://rvb-books.com/cdn/shop/products/packshot_b729640b-590c-4a2b-b72d-3a755860b094_{width}x.jpg?v=1605786311;https://rvb-books.com/cdn/shop/products/interieur2_a57f885d-0753-415f-8cd7-71f800a70a71_{width}x.jpg?v=1605786311|https://rvb-books.com/cdn/shop/products/interieur3copie_272a2580-4aeb-46eb-8485-38daaa7f786a_{width}x.jpg?v=1605786312|https://rvb-books.com/cdn/shop/products/interieur4copie_849bc429-14bb-4731-9a02-69129d122dbb_{width}x.jpg?v=1605786312|https://rvb-books.com/cdn/shop/products/interieur1_1abdbaf1-bf4e-4819-9f9b-ef94f6beb680_{width}x.jpg?v=1605786312;sold-out;https://rvb-books.com/products/erik-kessels-unfinished-father

column1: Stéphanie Solinas realized the Déserteurs project at the Père-Lachaise Cemetery in Paris. It is a response to the works of three famous men who are buried there: Valentin Haüy (1745-1822)
column2: the first school teacher for the blind
column3: Félix Tournachon known as Nadar (1820-1910)
column4: the famous portrait photographer
column5: and Alphonse Bertillon (1853-1914)
column6: the inventor of judiciary anthropometry.

column1: Déserteurs draws an inventory of what remains of the photographic portraits of the dead that adorn the tombs in the cemetery and that have been destroyed by time. Walking through the Père-Lachaise with its seventy thousand sepulchres
column2: the artist has identified and photographed three hundred and seventy nine “disappearances”. On each image
column3: she has engraved the coordinates of the geo-localisation of the tomb
column4: using the tactile system of writing
column5: Braille
column6: on the actual photographic print – like a call to “reanimate” the disappearing identities by a visit
column7: by a glance. After wrapping the photographs individually in conservation paper
column8: the artist has arranged the collection in a piled form becoming a sculpture.

column2: Stéphanie Solinas submits identities to the experience of the powers of classification. She has entrusted RVB Books with the task of selecting one hundred images to be reproduced in a special edition of one hundred books. Each book is unique because it conceals a different “disappearance”
column3: on which the artist has sculpted the Braille. Through this intervention
column4: she links each purchaser with a specific identity

Ruth van Beek has been collecting photographs from all over the place for years. Newspaper clippings
column2: amateur family pictures
column3: images from old books and the internet. She puts them together in an ever-growing archive. The archive is chaotic and fluent. Subjects range from family life
column4: landscape
column5: archeological findings
column6: natural disasters
column7: spectacular findings
column8: flower arrangement and handcraft instructions. The Hibernators are an animal species made from pictures of small common pets. Cats
column9: guinea pigs
column10: rabbits and dogs. The kind you find in many homes and that are beloved and pampered by their owners. Animals in the photos are brought back to life by cutting and folding the paper. Instead of being released
column11: they are restrained once more in a new shape. This way they become creatures that are silent like stones
column12: but that also show a tension. The moment before awaking. They want to move
